payers.name: Blue Cross Blue Shield of Massachusetts

classes.name: Metformin (biguanide)
classes.description: Biguanide oral therapy (metformin IR/ER) — guideline first-line pharmacotherapy for type 2 diabetes.
classes.plainDescription: The standard first pill for type 2 diabetes. Cheap, effective, decades of use.

classes.name: GLP-1 receptor agonists
classes.description: GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, dulaglutide, liraglutide) and the dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist tirzepatide — for type 2 diabetes with weight and cardiovascular benefit.
classes.plainDescription: Injectable (usually weekly) diabetes medicines that also help with weight. Most plans require approval first.

classes.name: SGLT2 inhibitors
classes.description: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozin) — oral therapy with heart-failure and kidney benefit.
classes.plainDescription: A once-a-day pill that lowers blood sugar and protects the heart and kidneys.
classes.indication: Type 2 diabetes; heart failure & CKD benefit

classes.name: Insulins (basal & rapid)
classes.description: Basal (glargine, degludec) and rapid-acting (lispro, aspart) insulins; formulary preference is brand-specific on most plans.
classes.plainDescription: Daily long-acting and mealtime insulins. Which exact brand is covered differs plan to plan.

preferredAgent.sig: Take 1 tablet (500mg) by mouth twice daily with meals
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one 500mg tablet by mouth twice a day, with food.

alternatives.note: Covered, M90 allowed, no PA for the standard XR tablet formulation

paRequired.drug: metformin extended-release (gastric/osmotic tablet formulations, e.g. Fortamet/Glumetza-type osmotic release)
paRequired.reason: Non-standard ER delivery formulations require PA; documentation of inadequate response (≥90 days therapy) or adverse reaction to standard ER tablet needed
Liquid formulation requires medical necessity documentation for members ≥13 years
Extended-release liquid formulation; PA required.

records.stepTherapy: For liquid metformin formulations, medical necessity documentation is required. For gestational diabetes use, PA requires documentation of inadequate response, adverse reaction, or contraindication to insulin therapy.
records.tier: No explicit dollar/tier language in MHDL; generic metformin IR and standard XR tablets are covered without PA (functionally first-line/preferred), all designated M90 (mandatory 90-day supply).
records.verificationNote: Read directly from the MassHealth Drug List (MHDL) Table 26: Antidiabetic Agents (pubtheradetail.do?id=26), last revised 07/2026, original effective 05/2003. Confirmed metformin immediate-release (500/850/1000mg) and standard XR tablet formulations are available without PA and carry mandatory 90-day supply designation; non-standard ER formulations (gastric/osmotic), the immediate-release oral solution for members ≥13, and Riomet ER require PA with specific inadequate-response criteria (≥90 days therapy) or adverse-reaction documentation. The 500mg BID sig is a standard representative starting dose, not verbatim from the MHDL page (which documents coverage/PA status by formulation, not detailed dosing).

preferredAgent.sig: Inject 1mg subcutaneously once weekly
preferredAgent.plainSig: Inject 1mg under the skin once a week, using the pen.

records.preferredRestriction: All GLP-1 agents, including the preferred agents, require PA. A SmartPA pathway lets claims process at the pharmacy without a manual PA request if the member has a history of at least 84 days of therapy with a GLP-1/GIP/GLP-1 agonist within the prior 120 days.

alternatives.note: Preferred drug (PD); requires PA like all GLP-1 agents, quantity limit one pen carton per 28 days
Preferred drug (PD); requires PA, quantity limit one pen carton per 28 days

paRequired.drug: liraglutide (Victoza) and other non-preferred GLP-1 injectables
paRequired.reason: Not on the preferred drug list; PA requires appropriate T2D diagnosis and not combined with another GLP-1 receptor agonist
All GLP-1 agents require PA regardless of preferred status
PA required with the same type-2-diabetes criteria.

records.stepTherapy: For prediabetes indication, PA requires documentation of inadequate response to metformin at a daily dose of 2,000mg (defined as A1c > 5.7%) or an adverse reaction with mitigation attempts. For type 2 diabetes, PA requires an appropriate diagnosis and confirmation the requested agent will not be combined with another GLP-1 receptor agonist.
records.tier: No explicit dollar/tier language in MHDL; dulaglutide, semaglutide injection, and tirzepatide are marked "Preferred Drug (PD)" but all require PA (SmartPA can auto-approve based on 84 days of recent therapy history).
records.verificationNote: Read directly from the MassHealth Drug List (MHDL) Table 26: Antidiabetic Agents (pubtheradetail.do?id=26), last revised 07/2026, original effective 05/2003. Confirmed dulaglutide (Trulicity), semaglutide injection (Ozempic), and tirzepatide (Mounjaro) are the three agents marked Preferred Drug (PD), that ALL GLP-1 agents require PA with no PA-free pathway, and the SmartPA auto-approval mechanism (84 days of therapy in prior 120 days) plus quantity limits (one pen carton per 28-day supply for Mounjaro/Trulicity, one pen per 30-day supply for Ozempic). The choice of semaglutide/Ozempic as the single "preferred agent" for this checkpoint (rather than listing all three as co-preferred) is an inference for schema purposes — the source treats all three as equally preferred, so this is marked partial for that specific choice, though the PA/preferred-status facts themselves are verified.

preferredAgent.sig: Take 1 tablet (10mg) by mouth once daily in the morning
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one 10mg tablet by mouth once a day, in the morning.

paRequired.reason: Requires documentation of inadequate response (≥90 days within 120-day period) to metformin plus dapagliflozin or Jardiance, quantity ≤1 unit/day.
Brand-preferred-over-generic (BOGL) is active for this agent specifically; requires PA.
Requires PA.
Requires PA.

records.stepTherapy: PA for non-preferred SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g., Invokana) requires inadequate response (≥90 days of therapy within a 120-day period) to metformin used in combination with dapagliflozin or empagliflozin (Jardiance).
records.tier: No explicit dollar/tier language in MHDL; empagliflozin (Jardiance) is the sole preferred drug in this class and is covered without PA.
records.verificationNote: Read directly from the MassHealth Drug List (MHDL) Table 26: Antidiabetic Agents (pubtheradetail.do?id=26), last revised 07/2026, original effective 05/2003. Confirmed empagliflozin (Jardiance) is the single preferred SGLT2 inhibitor with no PA required, while canagliflozin, dapagliflozin, ertugliflozin, and sotagliflozin all require PA with the specific step-therapy criterion of ≥90 days inadequate response to metformin + dapagliflozin or Jardiance combination therapy. Dapagliflozin is separately noted as brand-preferred-over-generic (BOGL active) in the source. The 10mg dose is a standard representative strength for empagliflozin, not verbatim dosing text from the page.

preferredAgent.sig: Inject subcutaneously per prescriber-directed dose and schedule
preferredAgent.plainSig: Inject under the skin at the dose and times your doctor tells you.

records.boglNote: Insulin degludec (Tresiba) and insulin glargine (Toujeo) are explicitly noted "Brand preferred over generic" in the MHDL antidiabetic table.

paRequired.reason: Brand preferred over generic; SmartPA allows auto-approval with ≥90 days of therapy in the last 120 days.

records.stepTherapy: Basal: PA requires medical necessity for use of the requested agent instead of insulin glargine prefilled syringe or vial (SmartPA can auto-approve Toujeo/Tresiba with ≥90 days of therapy in the last 120 days). Rapid-acting: PA requires inadequate response (≥90 days of therapy within a 180-day period), adverse reaction, or contraindication to insulin lispro (Humalog or a therapeutically equivalent generic).
records.tier: No explicit dollar/tier language in MHDL; insulin glargine (generic Lantus) is the preferred basal agent and insulin lispro (Humalog standard formulation) is the preferred rapid-acting agent, both covered without PA.
records.verificationNote: Read directly from the MassHealth Drug List (MHDL) Table 26: Antidiabetic Agents (pubtheradetail.do?id=26), last revised 07/2026, original effective 05/2003. Confirmed insulin glargine (generic Lantus) is preferred for basal insulin and insulin lispro (Humalog standard formulation) is preferred for rapid-acting, both without PA; all other basal agents (Tresiba, Rezvoglar, Semglee, Toujeo) and rapid-acting agents (Fiasp, Novolog, Merilog, Kirsty, Lyumjev/Lyumjev Tempo, Admelog, Apidra) require PA with documented step-therapy criteria. Tresiba and Toujeo are explicitly marked "Brand preferred over generic" (BOGL) in the source, and both have a SmartPA auto-approval pathway. Combining basal and rapid-acting into a single checkpoint record per the schema's single-preferredAgent shape is a structural simplification — both categories' facts are independently verified from the same table.

records.stepTherapy: This class is named in the Step Therapy 'Diabetes Management' list, which requires trying a first-step agent (e.g., generic metformin) before step-2 diabetes drugs (sulfonylureas, DPP-4s, SGLT2/GLP-1 combos, etc.) are covered — the guide lists step-therapy-affected drug names but does not print the underlying clinical criteria text.
records.verificationNote: Read the Diabetes Management step-therapy list (PDF page 22, printed page 20) and the Non-Covered Medications list (PDF pages 30-39). Confirmed: bare 'Metformin' (standard immediate-release generic) does not appear on Non-Covered, PA, or QCD lists, and is only referenced structurally as the implied first-step agent in the Diabetes Management step-therapy category (which lists step-2/non-preferred agents like Actos, Januvia, Farxiga, Jardiance, etc., not metformin itself). Metformin ER generic, Glucophage/Glucophage XR brand, and Riomet/Riomet ER (liquid) all appear in the step-therapy list as covered options; Fortamet, Glumetza brands and the two odd-strength 'Metformin 625mg'/'750mg' SKUs are separately non-covered. No PA is required for standard-strength generic metformin. Preferred agent and its clean-covered status are read directly off the document; sig/dosing details are standard clinical convention (not printed in this administrative guide).

records.verificationNote: Read the Prior Authorization alphabetical list (PDF pages 16-20, printed pages 14-18) and Non-Covered Medications list (PDF pages 30-39) plus the front-cover plan description confirming GLP-1 coverage is included on this specific formulary variant. Confirmed: Ozempic, Rybelsus, Trulicity, Mounjaro, Wegovy, Saxenda, Zepbound all appear on the Prior Authorization list (every branded GLP-1/dual-agonist requires PA under this plan). Victoza, Byetta, Bydureon, Bydureon Bcise, Adlyxin, Soliqua, and Xultophy appear on the Non-Covered list instead. The document does not print PA clinical criteria (e.g., A1c threshold, prior metformin trial) or rank Ozempic vs Mounjaro vs Trulicity as 'preferred' within the PA-required set — Ozempic is inferred as the de facto preferred/most commonly prescribed agent per market convention, not a rank stated in the source. No generic/biosimilar GLP-1 exists as of this review.

records.tier: Non-preferred / step-therapy tier (specific tier level not printed per-drug)
records.verificationNote: Read the Step Therapy section (PDF pages 21-22, printed pages 19-20), which has both a 'Cardiovascular Medications' list (Dapagliflozin, Farxiga, Inpefa, Jardiance, Verquvo) and a 'Diabetes Management' list naming essentially every SGLT2 agent and SGLT2 combo product, plus cross-checked the Non-Covered list for combo products. Confirmed both Farxiga (dapagliflozin) and Jardiance (empagliflozin) require step therapy under this plan for at least one indication — this is a class-wide step-therapy program, not a single clean-covered preferred agent. Farxiga is inferred as the likely first-choice/most commonly used SGLT2 since the source does not itself rank Farxiga vs Jardiance vs Invokana. No generic SGLT2 inhibitor exists as of this review (all are still branded).

records.verificationNote: Read the Quality Care Dosing list (PDF pages 10-15, printed pages 8-13) and Non-Covered Medications list (PDF pages 30-39). Confirmed Lantus, Novolog, Humalog, Novolin, Humulin, and Tresiba all appear only under Quality Care Dosing (a quantity/dose limit, not a coverage barrier) — meaning they are covered without PA. By contrast, Basaglar, Semglee, Rezvoglar (glargine alternatives), Levemir (detemir), Fiasp, Lyumjev, Admelog (rapid-acting alternatives), and the bare unbranded 'Insulin Aspart'/'Insulin Glargine'/'Insulin Lispro' line items all appear on the Non-Covered list. This is a brand-name-driven formulary (Lantus/Novolog/Humalog preferred over their follow-on-brand and biosimilar/authorized-generic competitors), unusual relative to typical genericfirst formularies — flagged as 'partial' rather than 'verified' because the guide does not explicitly label Lantus/Novolog/Humalog as 'preferred,' this is inferred from the absence of any restriction combined with competitors' explicit non-covered status.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from 'Diabetes - Antidiabetic Agents' (PDF pages 84-85, effective Jan. 1, 2026, 'Last Update: 06/15/26'). Ozempic subcutaneous solution pen-injector is Tier 2 with 'PA; QL: 1 pen (3 mL) per 28 days' printed verbatim in the LIMITATIONS column — confirming PA/QL apply even to this class's most commonly prescribed agent, so it is captured as the de facto formulary-preferred (lowest brand tier, injectable route matching typical first-line GLP-1 use) despite the restriction. Trulicity, Rybelsus, Mounjaro, and Xultophy rows were each individually read with their own PA/QL text. Victoza, Wegovy, Saxenda, Zepbound, and Bydureon were confirmed absent via full-text search of the extracted document (case-insensitive), and no anti-obesity/weight-loss category exists in the document's table of drug-class section headers, supporting a 'nonformulary/excluded' conclusion for weight-management GLP-1s rather than an oversight.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the 'Diabetes - Antidiabetic Agents' section (PDF pages 92-93, extracted text lines ~7220-7404) of the HPHC Select 3-Tier 2026 Prescription Drug List. 'metformin hcl oral tablet 1000 mg, 500 mg, 850 mg' (Generic Glucophage) is Tier 1 with only an 'HSA' note -- no PA/ST. The plain extended-release generic ('metformin hcl er oral tablet extended release 24 hour 500 mg' and '750 mg', noted 'Generic Glucophage XR') is also Tier 1 with no PA. However two other ER formulations are flagged 'PA; HSA' at line-level: 'metformin hcl er (mod)' (modified-release, Tier 2, PA) and 'metformin hcl er (osm)' (osmotic-release, generic Fortamet, Tier 1 but still PA-flagged per the document's own note column). Metformin combination products (glipizide-metformin, glyburide-metformin, pioglitazone-metformin, and all the branded combo tablets like Janumet/Synjardy/Xigduo) are listed separately and are out of scope for this plain-metformin class. Cover page: 'Created: October 1, 2025'; content pages: 'Last Update: 06/15/26'.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the 'Diabetes - Antidiabetic Agents' section (PDF pages 92-93, extracted text lines ~7220-7404) of the HPHC Select 3-Tier 2026 Prescription Drug List. Every GLP-1 agent that appears (OZEMPIC injectable and oral, MOUNJARO, TRULICITY, RYBELSUS) is Tier 2 and carries 'PA' plus a quantity limit in the notes column -- confirmed verbatim, e.g. 'OZEMPIC SUBCUTANEOUS SOLUTION PEN-INJECTOR ... 2 / PA; QL: 1 pen (3 mL) per 28 days; HSA'. No GLP-1 has a generic (none exist industry-wide as of this date for injectable semaglutide/tirzepatide/dulaglutide), so genericAvailable is correctly false for all. A targeted string search across the full extracted document text confirmed WEGOVY, ZEPBOUND, VICTOZA, BYETTA, BYDUREON, SAXENDA do not appear anywhere -- treated as non-formulary/excluded rather than fabricated as covered. Semaglutide (Ozempic) was selected as the representative preferred agent since it is the most commonly prescribed GLP-1 for type 2 diabetes in this list, though the formulary does not explicitly rank agents within the class -- all listed GLP-1s carry the identical PA+QL restriction, so 'preferred' here reflects typical prescribing frequency, not a formulary-stated preference. Cover page: 'Created: October 1, 2025'; content pages: 'Last Update: 06/15/26'.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the 'Diabetes - Antidiabetic Agents' section (PDF pages 92-93, extracted text lines ~7220-7404) of the HPHC Select 3-Tier 2026 Prescription Drug List. Generic 'dapagliflozin oral tablet 10 mg, 5 mg' is Tier 2 with only 'HSA; DIAB' notes -- no PA/ST/QL. Brand 'FARXIGA ORAL TABLET 10 MG, 5 MG' is also Tier 2, no PA. 'JARDIANCE ORAL TABLET 10 MG, 25 MG' (empagliflozin, brand-only, no generic yet) is Tier 2, no PA. 'GLYXAMBI' (empagliflozin-linagliptin combo) is Tier 2, no PA. Canagliflozin/Invokana and ertugliflozin/Steglatro were searched across the full document text and do not appear at all -- absent, not fabricated. Dapagliflozin was chosen as representative preferred agent given its generic availability (lower cost) vs Jardiance which remains brand-only; the formulary itself places both at the identical Tier 2/no-restriction level so this is a cost-based judgment call, not a formulary-stated preference. Cover page: 'Created: October 1, 2025'; content pages: 'Last Update: 06/15/26'.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the 'Diabetes - Insulins' section (PDF pages 96-99, extracted text lines ~7640-8196) of the HPHC Select 3-Tier 2026 Prescription Drug List. LANTUS SOLOSTAR and LANTUS U-100 VIAL are both Tier 2 with only 'AM' (affordability mandate) and 'HSA; DIAB' notes -- no PA. TOUJEO (glargine U-300) is likewise Tier 2, no PA. Rapid-acting: generic 'INSULIN LISPRO' (multiple pen/vial forms) and brand HUMALOG are Tier 2, no PA; LYUMJEV is Tier 2, no PA. By contrast, every NOVOLOG (aspart) and NOVOLIN (human insulin: N, R, 70/30) line item is explicitly Tier 3 with 'PA; HSA; DIAB' -- confirmed verbatim across ~9 separate NOVOLOG/NOVOLIN entries (FlexPen, Vial, Penfill, ReliOn store-brand variants). HUMULIN N/R and the U-500 concentrated form are Tier 2, no PA. No basal or rapid insulin has a true small-molecule generic on the market (Lilly's authorized-generic-style 'insulin lispro' line is the closest analog and is treated here as the generic-equivalent alternative, consistent with clinical/FDA labeling conventions) so genericAvailable is false for the Lantus/glargine entry itself. Cover page: 'Created: October 1, 2025'; content pages: 'Last Update: 06/15/26'.

preferredAgent.sig: Take 1 tablet (500 mg) by mouth twice daily with meals
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one 500 mg tablet by mouth twice a day with food

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the MGB Health Plan 2026 6-Tier Pharmacy Formulary PDF (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/192/20266T.pdf, header 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026', PDF metadata mod date 2026-06-30). The document lists two metformin lines: 'metFORMIN HCl ER' = Tier 1, and 'metFORMIN HCl Oral Tablet 1000 MG, 500 MG, 850 MG' = Tier 1 -- both with no PA, ST, QL, or SP notes. No brand name (Glucophage, Glucophage XR, Fortamet, Glumetza) appears as a separate formulary line, consistent with metformin being fully generic and off-patent; the plan simply lists the generic molecule. Immediate-release 500 mg was chosen as the representative preferred strength/form per standard first-line titration dosing; the ER form is listed as an alternative at the same tier.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the MGB Health Plan 2026 6-Tier Pharmacy Formulary PDF (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/192/20266T.pdf, header 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026', PDF metadata mod date 2026-06-30). Every GLP-1 agent in the document carries a PA flag with no exception -- there is no clean 'preferred, no-PA' agent in this class for MGB commercial. Confirmed line items: 'Ozempic (0.25 or 0.5 MG/DOSE)' / 'Ozempic (1 MG/DOSE)' / 'Ozempic (2 MG/DOSE)' = Tier 3, PA + QL 1 pen/28 days; 'Trulicity Subcutaneous Solution Auto-Injector' = Tier 3, PA + QL 4 pens/28 days; 'Mounjaro Subcutaneous Solution Auto-Injector' = Tier 3, PA + QL 4 pens/28 days; 'Liraglutide' (Victoza) = Tier 2, PA + QL 3 pens/28 days; 'Rybelsus' = Tier 3, PA + QL 30 EA/30 days. Weight-management-labeled products (Saxenda, Wegovy, Zepbound) carry an additional explicit note: 'Coverage Varies (Coverage for GLP-1s that share an indication for weight management or obesity depends on the member's specific plan. Plan documents outline exclusions.)' on top of the PA/QL flags -- distinct and more restrictive than the diabetes-indicated agents, so those three are placed in paRequired rather than alternatives. Semaglutide (Ozempic) was selected as the representative preferred/most commonly prescribed diabetes GLP-1, but its PA requirement is captured explicitly in preferredRestriction since no agent in this class is PA-free. Byetta, Bydureon, and Adlyxin (exenatide/lixisenatide) do not appear anywhere in the 134-page document -- confirmed absent (non-formulary), not simply mis-parsed, by checking the alphabetical neighborhood around 'B' and 'A' entries.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the MGB Health Plan 2026 6-Tier Pharmacy Formulary PDF (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/192/20266T.pdf, header 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026', PDF metadata mod date 2026-06-30). 'Dapagliflozin' (brand ref Farxiga) is listed at Tier 2 with no PA/ST/QL flag -- the generic is fully substitutable and unrestricted. 'Farxiga' also appears as its own separate line at Tier 3 (the brand-name formulary entry, used when a pharmacy dispenses brand rather than the AB-rated generic). 'Jardiance' (empagliflozin, no generic available in 2026) = Tier 3, no PA/ST/QL flag -- clean covered, just non-preferred cost-sharing vs. the Tier 2 generic. 'Dapaglifloz Base-metFORMIN ER' (generic Xigduo XR) = Tier 2, no restriction. Searched exhaustively for 'canagliflozin', 'ertugliflozin', 'Invokana', and 'Steglatro' across the full 134-page extracted text with zero matches -- confirmed genuinely absent from the formulary (not a parsing gap, verified by checking neighboring alphabetical entries render correctly), so both are marked nonformulary rather than fabricated as PA/step.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the MGB Health Plan 2026 6-Tier Pharmacy Formulary PDF (fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/192/20266T.pdf, header 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026', PDF metadata mod date 2026-06-30). Confirmed clean Tier 2 with no PA/ST/QL notes for: 'Lantus' and 'Lantus SoloStar' (glargine), 'Toujeo SoloStar' and 'Toujeo Max SoloStar' (glargine U-300), 'HumaLOG Injection/Junior KwikPen/KwikPen/Mix 75-25/Mix 50-50' (lispro), 'Lyumjev' and 'Lyumjev KwikPen' (lispro-aabc), and 'HumuLIN 70/30', 'HumuLIN N', 'HumuLIN R U-500' (human insulin). Notably, this formulary carries only Sanofi (Lantus, Toujeo) and Lilly (Humalog, Lyumjev, Humulin) insulin products -- an exhaustive text search for 'novo', 'levemir', 'tresiba', 'aspart', 'degludec', 'detemir', 'basaglar', 'semglee', 'rezvoglar', and 'admelog' across the full 134-page extracted document returned zero matches. This was double-checked by confirming the alphabetical neighborhood renders correctly (e.g. 'Novoeight' and 'NovoSeven RT', both Novo Nordisk hemophilia products, DO appear near where NovoLog would sit, and 'Norethindrone'/'Nortriptyline' entries confirm the 'N' section extracted cleanly) -- so the absence is a genuine formulary exclusion of the entire Novo Nordisk insulin line plus glargine/lispro biosimilars and Lilly's Basaglar/Admelog, not a PDF parsing gap. Insulin glargine (Lantus) was chosen as the representative preferred basal agent and insulin lispro (Humalog) noted as the rapid-acting alternative since the formulary does not single out one over the other -- all Sanofi/Lilly insulins listed are equally Tier 2/unrestricted.
